elements.sent-2411-001: Caffarelli's contraction theorem bounds the derivative of the optimal transport map between a log-convex measure and a strongly log-concave measure.
elements.sent-2411-002: We show that an analogous phenomenon holds on the level of the trace: The trace of the derivative of the optimal transport map between a log-subharmonic measure and a strongly log-concave measure is bounded.
elements.sent-2411-003: We show that this trace bound has a number of consequences pertaining to volume-contracting transport maps, majorization and its monotonicity along Wasserstein geodesics, growth estimates of log-subharmonic functions, the Wehrl conjecture for Glauber states, and two-dimensional Coulomb gases.
elements.sent-2411-004: We also discuss volume-contraction properties for the Kim-Milman transport map.

elements.subsection-2411-004: The Wehrl conjecture for Glauber states
elements.sent-2411-091: Next we apply our majorization results to provide a new proof and stability results for the Wehrl conjecture for Glauber states.
elements.sent-2411-092: The Wehrl entropy is a classical notion of entropy defined for quantum mechanical states.
elements.sent-2411-093: Wehrl presented this notion in \cite{wehrl1979relation and conjectured that this entropy is bounded by 1 (in dimension 1).}
elements.sent-2411-094: The conjecture was proven by Lieb \cite{MR0506364 , and the characterization of the minimizers was established by Carlen \cite{MR1105661 .}}
elements.sent-2411-095: The validity of Wehrl-type conjectures in other settings has been actively investigated, but there are still questions which remain open \cite{kulikov2022monotonicity, MR4580699 .}
elements.sent-2411-096: In this section we propose a new transport approach towards this problem.
elements.sent-2411-097: In particular, we will provide another proof of the (generalized) Wehrl conjecture for Glauber states using volume-contracting transport maps, as well as stability results.
elements.sent-2411-098: We will now present the setting of the original Wehrl conjecture following \cite{MR1105661 .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Fix $h>0$ to be the Planck constant and let $\hbar:=\frac{h {2\pi}$ be the reduced Planck constant.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Given a Schr\"odinger wave function $\wave\in L^2(\R^d,\diff x)$ define the coherent state transform $\cotrans:L^2(\R^d,\diff x)\to L^2(\R^{2d ,h^{-d}\diff q\diff p)$, where $\diff x,\diff q,\diff p$ denote the standard Lebesgue measure on $\R^{d}$, by \begin{equation}\label{eq:conerent_transform_def} \cotrans \wave(q,p):=e^{\frac{i}{2\hbar}\langle q,p\rangle}\int_{\R^{d}}e^{\frac{i}{\hbar}\langle x,p\rangle}e^{-\frac{|x-q|^2}{2\hbar}}\wave(x)\diff x.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-101: The coherent state transform maps a wave function to a function on the phase space, whose modulus $|\cotrans \wave(q,p)|^2$ represents the density (not necessarily normalized) of phase space states.
elements.sent-2411-102: The coherent state transform is isometric, so if $|\psi|_{L^2(\R^{d ,\diff x) =1$, hence \begin{equation}\label{eq:phase_prob_def} \prob_{\wave}(q,p):=h^{-d}|\cotrans \wave(q,p)|^2\end{equation} is a probability measure on the phase space $\R^{2d}$.}}

elements.sent-2411-117: Coulomb gases are important models in mathematical physics with deep connections to random matrix theory; see \cite{MR4619310 for a brief survey.}
elements.sent-2411-118: In this section we review the two-dimensional (one-component) Coulomb gas, to which our results above will apply.
elements.sent-2411-119: We consider $N$ particles in $\R^2$ of identical charge in a fixed neutralizing background at inverse temperature $\beta$, whose interactions is logarithmic.
elements.sent-2411-120: We identify $\R^2 \simeq\C$ and denote the positions of the particles as $z:=(z_1,\ldots,z_N)\in\C^N$, for $z_i\in \C$.
elements.sent-2411-121: Let $\source_{\beta,N}$ be the probability measure on $\C^{N}$ defined by \begin{equation}\label{eq:mu_betaN} \diff \source_{\beta,N,Q}(z):=\frac{e^{-Q_{\beta,N}(z)-G_{\beta,N}(z)}}{\mathcal Z_{\beta, N}}\diff z\quad \text{with}\quad \mathcal Z_{\beta, N}:=\int_{\C^N}e^{-Q_{\beta,N}(z)-G_{\beta,N}(z)}\diff z,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}\label{eq:V_betaN} Q_{\beta,N}(z):=\beta N\sum_{j=1}^NQ(z_j)\quad\text{with}\quad Q:\C\to \R,\end{equation} and \begin{equation}\label{eq:V_betaN} G_{\beta,N}(z):=-\beta \sum_{i<j=1}^N\log |z_i-z_j|.\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-004: The Kim-Milman heat flow transport map
elements.sent-2411-282: When the target measure $\target$ is the standard Gaussian measure $\Gaussian$ we can construct a volume-contracting map based on the heat flow map of Kim and E.
elements.sent-2411-284: While this transport map exists outside the Gaussian setting \cite{MR2983070,MR4707029 , our techniques to establish volume contraction are restricted to Gaussian targets.}
elements.sent-2411-285: In addition, in contrast to the Brenier map, we can only establish volume contraction rather than control of the divergence of the map.
elements.sent-2411-286: Finally, to establish the existence of the heat flow map we require further regularity than those for the optimal transport map.
elements.sent-2411-287: For these reasons, we will keep this section brief and assume whatever regularity is needed.
elements.sent-2411-288: We recall the definition of the Orenstein-Uhlenbeck semigroup $(\Pheat_t)$, \begin{equation}\label{eq:OU_def} \Pheat_t\density(x):=\int_{\R^{\dd}} \density(e^{-t}x+\sqrt{1-e^{-2t}}y)\diff \Gaussian(y),\quad t\ge 0,\quad x\in \R^{\dd}, \quad \density \in L^1(\Gaussian).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-289: The construction of the heat flow map between a source measure $\source$ and a Gaussian target $\Gaussian$ is based on the following flow.
elements.sent-2411-290: Let $\prob$ be an absolutely continuous probability measure on $\R^{\dd}$, and let $\density:=\frac{\diff\prob {\diff\Gaussian}$.}
elements.sent-2411-291: Suppose the ordinary differential equation \begin{equation}\label{eq:KM_construction} \begin{cases} \partial_t\KM_t(x)=-\nabla\log\Pheat_t\density(\KM_t(x)),\quad \text{for} ~ t>0 \text{ and } x\in \R^{\dd}, \KM_0(x)=x,\quad \text{for}~ x\in \R^{\dd}, \end{cases}\end{equation} has a unique smooth solution.

elements.sent-2411-292: Then, the probability measures $\prob_t:=(\KM_t)_{\sharp}\prob$ satisfy \begin{equation}\label{eq:heat_flow_path} \diff\prob_t(x)=\Pheat_t \density(x)\diff\Gaussian(x),\quad\text{for } t\ge 0 \text{ and } x\in \R^{\dd}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-293: The identity \eqref{eq:heat_flow_path follows from the standard switch between the Eulerian and Lagrangian perspectives, as well as the partial differential equation satisfied by $(t,x)\mapsto \Pheat_t \density(x)$ (cf.. \cite[\S 1.2]{MR2983070 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-294: The key point is that as $t\to\infty$, $\Pheat_t \density\to 1$, so $\prob_t\to \Gaussian$.
elements.sent-2411-295: In particular, whenever $\KM:=\lim_{t\to\infty}\KM_t$ exists, we find that $\KM$ transports $\prob$ to $\Gaussian$.
elements.sent-2411-296: The map $\KM$ is the Kim-Milman heat flow map.

elements.subsection-2411-008: Smoothing under Ornstein-Uhlenbeck and heat semigroups
elements.sent-2411-330: In this section we discuss Proposition \ref{prop:OU_smooth .}
elements.sent-2411-331: Since most of the results in the proposition are standard, when relevant we will only sketch the arguments and give references for detailed proofs.
elements.sent-2411-332: For the sake of completeness we will prove some additional smoothing properties beyond those stated in Proposition \ref{prop:OU_smooth .}
